If `x` was a scalar, so\\n563:        is `out`, otherwise an array object is returned.\\n564: \\n565:     See Also\\n566:     --------\\n567:     numpy.arcsin\\n568: \\n569:     Notes\\n570:     -----\\n571:     For an arcsin() that returns ``NAN`` when real `x` is not in the\\n572:     interval ``[-1,1]``, use `numpy.arcsin`.\\n573: \\n574:     Examples\\n575:     --------\\n576:     >>> import numpy as np\\n577:     >>> np.set_printoptions(precision=4)\\n578: \\n579:     >>> np.emath.arcsin(0)\\n580:     0.0\\n581: \\n582:     >>> np.emath.arcsin([0,1])\\n583:     array([0.    , 1.5708])\\n584: \\n585:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n586:     x = _fix_real_abs_gt_1(x)\\n587:     return nx.arcsin(x)\\n588: \\n589: \\n590: @set_module('numpy.lib.scimath')\\n591: @array_function_dispatch(_unary_dispatcher)\\n592: def arctanh(x):\\n593:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n594:     Compute the inverse hyperbolic tangent of `x`.\\n595: \\n596:     Return the \\\"principal value\\\" (for a description of this, see\\n597:     `numpy.arctanh`) of ``arctanh(x)``. For real `x` such that\\n598:     ``abs(x) < 1``, this is a real number.  If `abs(x) > 1`, or if `x` is\\n599:     complex, the result is complex. Finally, `x = 1` returns``inf`` and\\n600:     ``x=-1`` returns ``-inf``.\\n601: \\n602:     Parameters\\n603:     ----------\\n604:     x : array_like\\n605:        The value(s) whose arctanh is (are) required.\\n606: \\n607:     Returns\\n608:     -------\\n609:     out : ndarray or scalar\\n610:        The inverse hyperbolic tangent(s) of the `x` value(s). If `x` was\\n611:        a scalar so is `out`, otherwise an array is returned.\\n612: \\n613: \\n614:     See Also\\n615:     --------\\n616:     numpy.arctanh\\n617: \\n618:     Notes\\n619:     -----\\n620:     For an arctanh() that returns ``NAN`` when real `x` is not in the\\n621:     interval ``(-1,1)``, use `numpy.arctanh` (this latter, however, does\\n622:     return +/-inf for ``x = +/-1``).\\n623: \\n624:     Examples\\n625:     --------\\n626:     >>> import numpy as np\\n627:     >>> np.set_printoptions(precision=4)\\n628: \\n629:     >>> np.emath.arctanh(0.5)\\n630:     0.5493061443340549\\n631: \\n632:     >>> from numpy.testing import suppress_warnings\\n633:     >>> with suppress_warnings() as sup:\\n634:     ...     sup.filter(RuntimeWarning)\\n635:     ...     np.emath.arctanh(np.eye(2))\\n636:     array([[inf,  0.],\\n637:            [ 0., inf]])\\n638:     >>> np.emath.arctanh([1j])\\n639:     array([0.+0.7854j])\\n640: \\n641:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n642:     x = _fix_real_abs_gt_1(x)\\n643:     return nx.arctanh(x)\\n644: \",\"url\":\"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/numpy--v2.2.2@9ca88bff35bf60de74f9f95e6f90e6b4e0913e78/-/blob/numpy/lib/_scimath_impl.py\"}"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":"toolu_01KcrzQMt8QSgtWj8vuYnku5","session_id":"3818d021-ff76-4cbe-9aef-54b9f8949d86","uuid":"b8b1ae62-1f42-4a51-955f-02f1daddf5cc"}
{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01A9rP6t7aocrsg4wKQHvc5y","type":"tool_result","content":"{\"content\":\"1: \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n2: The arraypad module contains a group of functions to pad values onto the edges\\n3: of an n-dimensional array.\\n4: \\n5: \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n6: import numpy as np\\n7: from numpy._core.overrides import array_function_dispatch\\n8: from numpy.lib._index_tricks_impl import ndindex\\n9: \\n10: \\n11: __all__ = ['pad']\\n12: \\n13: \\n14: ###############################################################################\\n15: # Private utility functions.\\n16: \\n17: \\n18: def _round_if_needed(arr, dtype):\\n19:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n20:     Rounds arr inplace if destination dtype is integer.\\n21: \\n22:     Parameters\\n23:     ----------\\n24:     arr : ndarray\\n25:         Input array.\\n26:     dtype : dtype\\n27:         The dtype of the destination array.\\n28:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n29:     if np.issubdtype(dtype, np.integer):\\n30:         arr.round(out=arr)\\n31: \\n32: \\n33: def _slice_at_axis(sl, axis):\\n34:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n35:     Construct tuple of slices to slice an array in the given dimension.\\n36: \\n37:     Parameters\\n38:     ----------\\n39:     sl : slice\\n40:         The slice for the given dimension.\\n41:     axis : int\\n42:         The axis to which `sl` is applied. All other dimensions are left\\n43:         \\\"unsliced\\\".\\n44: \\n45:     Returns\\n46:     -------\\n47:     sl : tuple of slices\\n48:         A tuple with slices matching `shape` in length.\\n49: \\n50:     Examples\\n51:     --------\\n52:     >>> np._slice_at_axis(slice(None, 3, -1), 1)\\n53:     (slice(None, None, None), slice(None, 3, -1), (...,))\\n54:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n55:     return (slice(None),) * axis + (sl,) + (...,)\\n56: \\n57: \\n58: def _view_roi(array, original_area_slice, axis):\\n59:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n60:     Get a view of the current region of interest during iterative padding.\\n61: \\n62:     When padding multiple dimensions iteratively corner values are\\n63:     unnecessarily overwritten multiple times. This function reduces the\\n64:     working area for the first dimensions so that corners are excluded.\\n65: \\n66:     Parameters\\n67:     ----------\\n68:     array : ndarray\\n69:         The array with the region of interest.\\n70:     original_area_slice : tuple of slices\\n71:         Denotes the area with original values of the unpadded array.\\n72:     axis : int\\n73:         The currently padded dimension assuming that `axis` is padded before\\n74:         `axis` + 1.\\n75: \\n76:     Returns\\n77:     -------\\n78:     roi : ndarray\\n79:         The region of interest of the original `array`.\\n80:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n81:     axis += 1\\n82:     sl = (slice(None),) * axis + original_area_slice[axis:]\\n83:     return array[sl]\\n84: \\n85: \\n86: def _pad_simple(array, pad_width, fill_value=None):\\n87:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n88:     Pad array on all sides with either a single value or undefined values.\\n89: \\n90:     Parameters\\n91:     ----------\\n92:     array : ndarray\\n93:         Array to grow.\\n94:     pad_width : sequence of tuple[int, int]\\n95:         Pad width on both sides for each dimension in `arr`.\\n96:     fill_value : scalar, optional\\n97:         If provided the padded area is filled with this value, otherwise\\n98:         the pad area left undefined.\\n99: \\n100:     Returns\\n101:     -------\\n102:     padded : ndarray\\n103:         The padded array with the same dtype as`array`. Its order will default\\n104:         to C-style if `array` is not F-contiguous.\\n105:     original_area_slice : tuple\\n106:         A tuple of slices pointing to the area of the original array.\\n107:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n108:     # Allocate grown array\\n109:     new_shape = tuple(\\n110:         left + size + right\\n111:         for size, (left, right) in zip(array.shape, pad_width)\\n112:     )\\n113:     order = 'F' if array.flags.fnc else 'C'  # Fortran and not also C-order\\n114:     padded = np.empty(new_shape, dtype=array.dtype, order=order)\\n115: \\n116:     if fill_value is not None:\\n117:         padded.fill(fill_value)\\n118: \\n119:     # Copy old array into correct space\\n120:     original_area_slice = tuple(\\n121:         slice(left, left + size)\\n122:         for size, (left, right) in zip(array.shape, pad_width)\\n123:     )\\n124:     padded[original_area_slice] = array\\n125: \\n126:     return padded, original_area_slice\\n127: \\n128: \\n129: def _set_pad_area(padded, axis, width_pair, value_pair):\\n130:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n131:     Set empty-padded area in given dimension.\\n132: \\n133:     Parameters\\n134:     ----------\\n135:     padded : ndarray\\n136:         Array with the pad area which is modified inplace.\\n137:     axis : int\\n138:         Dimension with the pad area to set.\\n139:     width_pair : (int, int)\\n140:         Pair of widths that mark the pad area on both sides in the given\\n141:         dimension.\\n142:     value_pair : tuple of scalars or ndarrays\\n143:         Values inserted into the pad area on each side. It must match or be\\n144:         broadcastable to the shape of `arr`.\\n145:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n146:     left_slice = _slice_at_axis(slice(None, width_pair[0]), axis)\\n147:     padded[left_slice] = value_pair[0]\\n148: \\n149:     right_slice = _slice_at_axis(\\n150:         slice(padded.shape[axis] - width_pair[1], None), axis)\\n151:     padded[right_slice] = value_pair[1]\\n152: \\n153: \\n154: def _get_edges(padded, axis, width_pair):\\n155:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n156:     Retrieve edge values from empty-padded array in given dimension.\\n157: \\n158:     Parameters\\n159:     ----------\\n160:     padded : ndarray\\n161:         Empty-padded array.\\n162:     axis : int\\n163:         Dimension in which the edges are considered.\\n164:     width_pair : (int, int)\\n165:         Pair of widths that mark the pad area on both sides in the given\\n166:         dimension.\\n167: \\n168:     Returns\\n169:     -------\\n170:     left_edge, right_edge : ndarray\\n171:         Edge values of the valid area in `padded` in the given dimension. Its\\n172:         shape will always match `padded` except for the dimension given by\\n173:         `axis` which will have a length of 1.\\n174:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n175:     left_index = width_pair[0]\\n176:     left_slice = _slice_at_axis(slice(left_index, left_index + 1), axis)\\n177:     left_edge = padded[left_slice]\\n178: \\n179:     right_index = padded.shape[axis] - width_pair[1]\\n180:     right_slice = _slice_at_axis(slice(right_index - 1, right_index), axis)\\n181:     right_edge = padded[right_slice]\\n182: \\n183:     return left_edge, right_edge\\n184: \\n185: \\n186: def _get_linear_ramps(padded, axis, width_pair, end_value_pair):\\n187:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n188:     Construct linear ramps for empty-padded array in given dimension.\\n189: \\n190:     Parameters\\n191:     ----------\\n192:     padded : ndarray\\n193:         Empty-padded array.\\n194:     axis : int\\n195:         Dimension in which the ramps are constructed.\\n196:     width_pair : (int, int)\\n197:         Pair of widths that mark the pad area on both sides in the given\\n198:         dimension.\\n199:     end_value_pair : (scalar, scalar)\\n200:         End values for the linear ramps which form the edge of the fully padded\\n201:         array. These values are included in the linear ramps.\\n202: \\n203:     Returns\\n204:     -------\\n205:     left_ramp, right_ramp : ndarray\\n206:         Linear ramps to set on both sides of `padded`.\\n207:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n208:     edge_pair = _get_edges(padded, axis, width_pair)\\n209: \\n210:     left_ramp, right_ramp = (\\n211:         np.linspace(\\n212:             start=end_value,\\n213:             stop=edge.squeeze(axis), # Dimension is replaced by linspace\\n214:             num=width,\\n215:             endpoint=False,\\n216:             dtype=padded.dtype,\\n217:             axis=axis\\n218:         )\\n219:         for end_value, edge, width in zip(\\n220:             end_value_pair, edge_pair, width_pair\\n221:         )\\n222:     )\\n223: \\n224:     # Reverse linear space in appropriate dimension\\n225:     right_ramp = right_ramp[_slice_at_axis(slice(None, None, -1), axis)]\\n226: \\n227:     return left_ramp, right_ramp\\n228: \\n229: \\n230: def _get_stats(padded, axis, width_pair, length_pair, stat_func):\\n231:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n232:     Calculate statistic for the empty-padded array in given dimension.\\n233: \\n234:     Parameters\\n235:     ----------\\n236:     padded : ndarray\\n237:         Empty-padded array.\\n238:     axis : int\\n239:         Dimension in which the statistic is calculated.\\n240:     width_pair : (int, int)\\n241:         Pair of widths that mark the pad area on both sides in the given\\n242:         dimension.\\n243:     length_pair : 2-element sequence of None or int\\n244:         Gives the number of values in valid area from each side that is\\n245:         taken into account when calculating the statistic. If None the entire\\n246:         valid area in `padded` is considered.\\n247:     stat_func : function\\n248:         Function to compute statistic. The expected signature is\\n249:         ``stat_func(x: ndarray, axis: int, keepdims: bool) -> ndarray``.\\n250: \\n251:     Returns\\n252:     -------\\n253:     left_stat, right_stat : ndarray\\n254:         Calculated statistic for both sides of `padded`.\\n255:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n256:     # Calculate indices of the edges of the area with original values\\n257:     left_index = width_pair[0]\\n258:     right_index = padded.shape[axis] - width_pair[1]\\n259:     # as well as its length\\n260:     max_length = right_index - left_index\\n261: \\n262:     # Limit stat_lengths to max_length\\n263:     left_length, right_length = length_pair\\n264:     if left_length is None or max_length < left_length:\\n265:         left_length = max_length\\n266:     if right_length is None or max_length < right_length:\\n267:         right_length = max_length\\n268: \\n269:     if (left_length == 0 or right_length == 0) \\\\\\n270:             and stat_func in {np.amax, np.amin}:\\n271:         # amax and amin can't operate on an empty array,\\n272:         # raise a more descriptive warning here instead of the default one\\n273:         raise ValueError(\\\"stat_length of 0 yields no value for padding\\\")\\n274: \\n275:     # Calculate statistic for the left side\\n276:     left_slice = _slice_at_axis(\\n277:         slice(left_index, left_index + left_length), axis)\\n278:     left_chunk = padded[left_slice]\\n279:     left_stat = stat_func(left_chunk, axis=axis, keepdims=True)\\n280:     _round_if_needed(left_stat, padded.dtype)\\n281: \\n282:     if left_length == right_length == max_length:\\n283:         # return early as right_stat must be identical to left_stat\\n284:         return left_stat, left_stat\\n285: \\n286:     # Calculate statistic for the right side\\n287:     right_slice = _slice_at_axis(\\n288:         slice(right_index - right_length, right_index), axis)\\n289:     right_chunk = padded[right_slice]\\n290:     right_stat = stat_func(right_chunk, axis=axis, keepdims=True)\\n291:     _round_if_needed(right_stat, padded.dtype)\\n292: \\n293:     return left_stat, right_stat\\n294: \\n295: \\n296: def _set_reflect_both(padded, axis, width_pair, method,\\n297:                       original_period, include_edge=False):\\n298:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n299:     Pad `axis` of `arr` with reflection.\\n300: \\n301:     Parameters\\n302:     ----------\\n303:     padded : ndarray\\n304:         Input array of arbitrary shape.\\n305:     axis : int\\n306:         Axis along which to pad `arr`.\\n307:     width_pair : (int, int)\\n308:         Pair of widths that mark the pad area on both sides in the given\\n309:         dimension.\\n310:     method : str\\n311:         Controls method of reflection; options are 'even' or 'odd'.\\n312:     original_period : int\\n313:         Original length of data on `axis` of `arr`.\\n314:     include_edge : bool\\n315:         If true, edge value is included in reflection, otherwise the edge\\n316:         value forms the symmetric axis to the reflection.\\n317: \\n318:     Returns\\n319:     -------\\n320:     pad_amt : tuple of ints, length 2\\n321:         New index positions of padding to do along the `axis`. If these are\\n322:         both 0, padding is done in this dimension.\\n323:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n324:     left_pad, right_pad = width_pair\\n325:     old_length = padded.shape[axis] - right_pad - left_pad\\n326: \\n327:     if include_edge:\\n328:         # Avoid wrapping with only a subset of the original area\\n329:         # by ensuring period can only be a multiple of the original\\n330:         # area's length.\\n331:         old_length = old_length // original_period * original_period\\n332:         # Edge is included, we need to offset the pad amount by 1\\n333:         edge_offset = 1\\n334:     else:\\n335:         # Avoid wrapping with only a subset of the original area\\n336:         # by ensuring period can only be a multiple of the original\\n337:         # area's length.\\n338:         old_length = ((old_length - 1) // (original_period - 1)\\n339:             * (original_period - 1) + 1)\\n340:         edge_offset = 0  # Edge is not included, no need to offset pad amount\\n341:         old_length -= 1  # but must be omitted from the chunk\\n342: \\n343:     if left_pad > 0:\\n344:         # Pad with reflected values on left side:\\n345:         # First limit chunk size which can't be larger than pad area\\n346:         chunk_length = min(old_length, left_pad)\\n347:         # Slice right to left, stop on or next to edge, start relative to stop\\n348:         stop = left_pad - edge_offset\\n349:         start = stop + chunk_length\\n350:         left_slice = _slice_at_axis(slice(start, stop, -1), axis)\\n351:         left_chunk = padded[left_slice]\\n352: \\n353:         if method == \\\"odd\\\":\\n354:             # Negate chunk and align with edge\\n355:             edge_slice = _slice_at_axis(slice(left_pad, left_pad + 1), axis)\\n356:             left_chunk = 2 * padded[edge_slice] - left_chunk\\n357: \\n358:         # Insert chunk into padded area\\n359:         start = left_pad - chunk_length\\n360:         stop = left_pad\\n361:         pad_area = _slice_at_axis(slice(start, stop), axis)\\n362:         padded[pad_area] = left_chunk\\n363:         # Adjust pointer to left edge for next iteration\\n364:         left_pad -= chunk_length\\n365: \\n366:     if right_pad > 0:\\n367:         # Pad with reflected values on right side:\\n368:         # First limit chunk size which can't be larger than pad area\\n369:         chunk_length = min(old_length, right_pad)\\n370:         # Slice right to left, start on or next to edge, stop relative to start\\n371:         start = -right_pad + edge_offset - 2\\n372:         stop = start - chunk_length\\n373:         right_slice = _slice_at_axis(slice(start, stop, -1), axis)\\n374:         right_chunk = padded[right_slice]\\n375: \\n376:         if method == \\\"odd\\\":\\n377:             # Negate chunk and align with edge\\n378:             edge_slice = _slice_at_axis(\\n379:                 slice(-right_pad - 1, -right_pad), axis)\\n380:             right_chunk = 2 * padded[edge_slice] - right_chunk\\n381: \\n382:         # Insert chunk into padded area\\n383:         start = padded.shape[axis] - right_pad\\n384:         stop = start + chunk_length\\n385:         pad_area = _slice_at_axis(slice(start, stop), axis)\\n386:         padded[pad_area] = right_chunk\\n387:         # Adjust pointer to right edge for next iteration\\n388:         right_pad -= chunk_length\\n389: \\n390:     return left_pad, right_pad\\n391: \\n392: \\n393: def _set_wrap_both(padded, axis, width_pair, original_period):\\n394:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n395:     Pad `axis` of `arr` with wrapped values.\\n396: \\n397:     Parameters\\n398:     ----------\\n399:     padded : ndarray\\n400:         Input array of arbitrary shape.\\n401:     axis : int\\n402:         Axis along which to pad `arr`.\\n403:     width_pair : (int, int)\\n404:         Pair of widths that mark the pad area on both sides in the given\\n405:         dimension.\\n406:     original_period : int\\n407:         Original length of data on `axis` of `arr`.\\n408: \\n409:     Returns\\n410:     -------\\n411:     pad_amt : tuple of ints, length 2\\n412:         New index positions of padding to do along the `axis`. If these are\\n413:         both 0, padding is done in this dimension.\\n414:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n415:     left_pad, right_pad = width_pair\\n416:     period = padded.shape[axis] - right_pad - left_pad\\n417:     # Avoid wrapping with only a subset of the original area by ensuring period\\n418:     # can only be a multiple of the original area's length.\\n419:     period = period // original_period * original_period\\n420: \\n421:     # If the current dimension of `arr` doesn't contain enough valid values\\n422:     # (not part of the undefined pad area) we need to pad multiple times.\\n423:     # Each time the pad area shrinks on both sides which is communicated with\\n424:     # these variables.\\n425:     new_left_pad = 0\\n426:     new_right_pad = 0\\n427: \\n428:     if left_pad > 0:\\n429:         # Pad with wrapped values on left side\\n430:         # First slice chunk from left side of the non-pad area.\\n431:         # Use min(period, left_pad) to ensure that chunk is not larger than\\n432:         # pad area.\\n433:         slice_end = left_pad + period\\n434:         slice_start = slice_end - min(period, left_pad)\\n435:         right_slice = _slice_at_axis(slice(slice_start, slice_end), axis)\\n436:         right_chunk = padded[right_slice]\\n437: \\n438:         if left_pad > period:\\n439:             # Chunk is smaller than pad area\\n440:             pad_area = _slice_at_axis(slice(left_pad - period, left_pad), axis)\\n441:             new_left_pad = left_pad - period\\n442:         else:\\n443:             # Chunk matches pad area\\n444:             pad_area = _slice_at_axis(slice(None, left_pad), axis)\\n445:         padded[pad_area] = right_chunk\\n446: \\n447:     if right_pad > 0:\\n448:         # Pad with wrapped values on right side\\n449:         # First slice chunk from right side of the non-pad area.\\n450:         # Use min(period, right_pad) to ensure that chunk is not larger than\\n451:         # pad area.\\n452:         slice_start = -right_pad - period\\n453:         slice_end = slice_start + min(period, right_pad)\\n454:         left_slice = _slice_at_axis(slice(slice_start, slice_end), axis)\\n455:         left_chunk = padded[left_slice]\\n456: \\n457:         if right_pad > period:\\n458:             # Chunk is smaller than pad area\\n459:             pad_area = _slice_at_axis(\\n460:                 slice(-right_pad, -right_pad + period), axis)\\n461:             new_right_pad = right_pad - period\\n462:         else:\\n463:             # Chunk matches pad area\\n464:             pad_area = _slice_at_axis(slice(-right_pad, None), axis)\\n465:         padded[pad_area] = left_chunk\\n466: \\n467:     return new_left_pad, new_right_pad\\n468: \\n469: \\n470: def _as_pairs(x, ndim, as_index=False):\\n471:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n472:     Broadcast `x` to an array with the shape (`ndim`, 2).\\n473: \\n474:     A helper function for `pad` that prepares and validates arguments like\\n475:     `pad_width` for iteration in pairs.\\n476: \\n477:     Parameters\\n478:     ----------\\n479:     x : {None, scalar, array-like}\\n480:         The object to broadcast to the shape (`ndim`, 2).\\n481:     ndim : int\\n482:         Number of pairs the broadcasted `x` will have.\\n483:     as_index : bool, optional\\n484:         If `x` is not None, try to round each element of `x` to an integer\\n485:         (dtype `np.intp`) and ensure every element is positive.\\n486: \\n487:     Returns\\n488:     -------\\n489:     pairs : nested iterables, shape (`ndim`, 2)\\n490:         The broadcasted version of `x`.\\n491: \\n492:     Raises\\n493:     ------\\n494:     ValueError\\n495:         If `as_index` is True and `x` contains negative elements.\\n496:         Or if `x` is not broadcastable to the shape (`ndim`, 2).\\n497:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n498:     if x is None:\\n499:         # Pass through None as a special case, otherwise np.round(x) fails\\n500:         # with an AttributeError\\n501:         return ((None, None),) * ndim\\n502: \\n503:     x = np.array(x)\\n504:     if as_index:\\n505:         x = np.round(x).astype(np.intp, copy=False)\\n506: \\n507:     if x.ndim < 3:\\n508:         # Optimization: Possibly use faster paths for cases where `x` has\\n509:         # only 1 or 2 elements. `np.broadcast_to` could handle these as well\\n510:         # but is currently slower\\n511: \\n512:         if x.size == 1:\\n513:             # x was supplied as a single value\\n514:             x = x.ravel()  # Ensure x[0] works for x.ndim == 0, 1, 2\\n515:             if as_index and x < 0:\\n516:                 raise ValueError(\\\"index can't contain negative values\\\")\\n517:             return ((x[0], x[0]),) * ndim\\n518: \\n519:         if x.size == 2 and x.shape != (2, 1):\\n520:             # x was supplied with a single value for each side\\n521:             # but except case when each dimension has a single value\\n522:             # which should be broadcasted to a pair,\\n523:             # e.g. [[1], [2]] -> [[1, 1], [2, 2]] not [[1, 2], [1, 2]]\\n524:             x = x.ravel()  # Ensure x[0], x[1] works\\n525:             if as_index and (x[0] < 0 or x[1] < 0):\\n526:                 raise ValueError(\\\"index can't contain negative values\\\")\\n527:             return ((x[0], x[1]),) * ndim\\n528: \\n529:     if as_index and x.min() < 0:\\n530:         raise ValueError(\\\"index can't contain negative values\\\")\\n531: \\n532:     # Converting the array with `tolist` seems to improve performance\\n533:     # when iterating and indexing the result (see usage in `pad`)\\n534:     return np.broadcast_to(x, (ndim, 2)).tolist()\\n535: \\n536: \\n537: def _pad_dispatcher(array, pad_width, mode=None, **kwargs):\\n538:     return (array,)\\n539: \\n540: \\n541: ###############################################################################\\n542: # Public functions\\n543: \\n544: \\n545: @array_function_dispatch(_pad_dispatcher, module='numpy')\\n546: def pad(array, pad_width, mode='constant', **kwargs):\\n547:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n548:     Pad an array.\\n549: \\n550:     Parameters\\n551:     ----------\\n552:     array : array_like of rank N\\n553:         The array to pad.\\n554:     pad_width : {sequence, array_like, int}\\n555:         Number of values padded to the edges of each axis.\\n556:         ``((before_1, after_1), ... (before_N, after_N))`` unique pad widths\\n557:         for each axis.\\n558:         ``(before, after)`` or ``((before, after),)`` yields same before\\n559:         and after pad for each axis.\\n560:         ``(pad,)`` or ``int`` is a shortcut for before = after = pad width\\n561:         for all axes.\\n562:     mode : str or function, optional\\n563:         One of the following string values or a user supplied function.\\n564: \\n565:         'constant' (default)\\n566:             Pads with a constant value.\\n567:         'edge'\\n568:             Pads with the edge values of array.\\n569:         'linear_ramp'\\n570:             Pads with the linear ramp between end_value and the\\n571:             array edge value.\\n572:         'maximum'\\n573:             Pads with the maximum value of all or part of the\\n574:             vector along each axis.\\n575:         'mean'\\n576:             Pads with the mean value of all or part of the\\n577:             vector along each axis.\\n578:         'median'\\n579:             Pads with the median value of all or part of the\\n580:             vector along each axis.\\n581:         'minimum'\\n582:             Pads with the minimum value of all or part of the\\n583:             vector along each axis.\\n584:         'reflect'\\n585:             Pads with the reflection of the vector mirrored on\\n586:             the first and last values of the vector along each\\n587:             axis.\\n588:         'symmetric'\\n589:             Pads with the reflection of the vector mirrored\\n590:             along the edge of the array.\\n591:         'wrap'\\n592:             Pads with the wrap of the vector along the axis.\\n593:             The first values are used to pad the end and the\\n594:             end values are used to pad the beginning.\\n595:         'empty'\\n596:             Pads with undefined values.\\n597: \\n598:         <function>\\n599:             Padding function, see Notes.\\n600:     stat_length : sequence or int, optional\\n601:         Used in 'maximum', 'mean', 'median', and 'minimum'.  Number of\\n602:         values at edge of each axis used to calculate the statistic value.\\n603: \\n604:         ``((before_1, after_1), ... (before_N, after_N))`` unique statistic\\n605:         lengths for each axis.\\n606: \\n607:         ``(before, after)`` or ``((before, after),)`` yields same before\\n608:         and after statistic lengths for each axis.\\n609: \\n610:         ``(stat_length,)`` or ``int`` is a shortcut for\\n611:         ``before = after = statistic`` length for all axes.\\n612: \\n613:         Default is ``None``, to use the entire axis.\\n614:     constant_values : sequence or scalar, optional\\n615:         Used in 'constant'.  The values to set the padded values for each\\n616:         axis.\\n617: \\n618:         ``((before_1, after_1), ... (before_N, after_N))`` unique pad constants\\n619:         for each axis.\\n620: \\n621:         ``(before, after)`` or ``((before, after),)`` yields same before\\n622:         and after constants for each axis.\\n623: \\n624:         ``(constant,)`` or ``constant`` is a shortcut for\\n625:         ``before = after = constant`` for all axes.\\n626: \\n627:         Default is 0.\\n628:     end_values : sequence or scalar, optional\\n629:         Used in 'linear_ramp'.  The values used for the ending value of the\\n630:         linear_ramp and that will form the edge of the padded array.\\n631: \\n632:         ``((before_1, after_1), ... (before_N, after_N))`` unique end values\\n633:         for each axis.\\n634: \\n635:         ``(before, after)`` or ``((before, after),)`` yields same before\\n636:         and after end values for each axis.\\n637: \\n638:         ``(constant,)`` or ``constant`` is a shortcut for\\n639:         ``before = after = constant`` for all axes.\\n640: \\n641:         Default is 0.\\n642:     reflect_type : {'even', 'odd'}, optional\\n643:         Used in 'reflect', and 'symmetric'.  The 'even' style is the\\n644:         default with an unaltered reflection around the edge value.  For\\n645:         the 'odd' style, the extended part of the array is created by\\n646:         subtracting the reflected values from two times the edge value.\\n647: \\n648:     Returns\\n649:     -------\\n650:     pad : ndarray\\n651:         Padded array of rank equal to `array` with shape increased\\n652:         according to `pad_width`.\\n653: \\n654:     Notes\\n655:     -----\\n656:     For an array with rank greater than 1, some of the padding of later\\n657:     axes is calculated from padding of previous axes.  This is easiest to\\n658:     think about with a rank 2 array where the corners of the padded array\\n659:     are calculated by using padded values from the first axis.\\n660: \\n661:     The padding function, if used, should modify a rank 1 array in-place. It\\n662:     has the following signature::\\n663: \\n664:         padding_func(vector, iaxis_pad_width, iaxis, kwargs)\\n665: \\n666:     where\\n667: \\n668:     vector : ndarray\\n669:         A rank 1 array already padded with zeros.  Padded values are\\n670:         vector[:iaxis_pad_width[0]] and vector[-iaxis_pad_width[1]:].\\n671:     iaxis_pad_width : tuple\\n672:         A 2-tuple of ints, iaxis_pad_width[0] represents the number of\\n673:         values padded at the beginning of vector where\\n674:         iaxis_pad_width[1] represents the number of values padded at\\n675:         the end of vector.\\n676:     iaxis : int\\n677:         The axis currently being calculated.\\n678:     kwargs : dict\\n679:         Any keyword arguments the function requires.\\n680: \\n681:     Examples\\n682:     --------\\n683: @array_function_dispatch(_ix__dispatcher)\\n35: def ix_(*args):\\n36:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n37:     Construct an open mesh from multiple sequences.\\n38: \\n39:     This function takes N 1-D sequences and returns N outputs with N\\n40:     dimensions each, such that the shape is 1 in all but one dimension\\n41:     and the dimension with the non-unit shape value cycles through all\\n42:     N dimensions.\\n43: \\n44:     Using `ix_` one can quickly construct index arrays that will index\\n45:     the cross product. ``a[np.ix_([1,3],[2,5])]`` returns the array\\n46:     ``[[a[1,2] a[1,5]], will be interpreted as boolean masks for the\\n53:         corresponding dimension (equivalent to passing in\\n54:         ``np.nonzero(boolean_sequence)``).\\n55: \\n56:     Returns\\n57:     -------\\n58:     out : tuple of ndarrays\\n59:         N arrays with N dimensions each, with N the number of input\\n60:         sequences. Together these arrays form an open mesh.\\n61: \\n62:     See Also\\n63:     --------\\n64:     ogrid, mgrid, meshgrid\\n65: \\n66:     Examples\\n67:     --------\\n68:     >>> import numpy as np\\n69:     >>> a = np.arange(10).reshape(2, 5)\\n70:     >>> a\\n71:     array([[0, 1, 2, 3, 4],\\n72:            [5, 6, 7, 8, 9]])\\n73:     >>> ixgrid = np.ix_([0, 1], [2, 4])\\n74:     >>> ixgrid\\n75:     (array([[0],\\n76:            [1]]), array([[2, 4]]))\\n77:     >>> ixgrid[0].shape, ixgrid[1].shape\\n78:     ((2, 1), (1, 2))\\n79:     >>> a[ixgrid]\\n80:     array([[2, 4],\\n81:            [7, 9]])\\n82: \\n83:     >>> ixgrid = np.ix_([True, True], [2, 4])\\n84:     >>> a[ixgrid]\\n85:     array([[2, 4],\\n86:            [7, 9]])\\n87:     >>> ixgrid = np.ix_([True, True], [False, False, True, False, True])\\n88:     >>> a[ixgrid]\\n89:     array([[2, 4],\\n90:            [7, 9]])\\n91: \\n92:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n93:     out = []\\n94:     nd = len(args)\\n95:     for k, new in enumerate(args):\\n96:         if not isinstance(new, _nx.ndarray):\\n97:             new = np.asarray(new)\\n98:             if new.size == 0:\\n99:                 # Explicitly type empty arrays to avoid float default\\n100:                 new = new.astype(_nx.intp)\\n101:         if new.ndim != 1:\\n102:             raise ValueError(\\\"Cross index must be 1 dimensional\\\")\\n103:         if issubdtype(new.dtype, _nx.bool):\\n104:             new, = new.nonzero()\\n105:         new = new.reshape((1,)*k + (new.size,) + (1,)*(nd-k-1))\\n106:         out.append(new)\\n107:     return tuple(out)\\n108: \\n109: \\n110: class nd_grid:\\n111:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n112:     Construct a multi-dimensional \\\"meshgrid\\\".\\n113: \\n114:     ``grid = nd_grid()`` creates an instance which will return a mesh-grid\\n115:     when indexed.  The dimension and number of the output arrays are equal\\n116:     to the number of indexing dimensions.  If the step length is not a\\n117:     complex number, then the stop is not inclusive.\\n118: \\n119:     However, if the step length is a **complex number** (e.g. 5j), then the\\n120:     integer part of its magnitude is interpreted as specifying the\\n121:     number of points to create between the start and stop values, where\\n122:     the stop value **is inclusive**.\\n123: \\n124:     If instantiated with an argument of ``sparse=True``, the mesh-grid is\\n125:     open (or not fleshed out) so that only one-dimension of each returned\\n126:     argument is greater than 1.\\n127: \\n128:     Parameters\\n129:     ----------\\n130:     sparse : bool, optional\\n131:         Whether the grid is sparse or not. Default is False.\\n132: \\n133:     Notes\\n134:     -----\\n135:     Two instances of `nd_grid` are made available in the NumPy namespace,\\n136:     `mgrid` and `ogrid`, approximately defined as::\\n137: \\n138:         mgrid = nd_grid(sparse=False)\\n139:         ogrid = nd_grid(sparse=True)\\n140: \\n141:     Users should use these pre-defined instances instead of using `nd_grid`\\n142:     directly.\\n143:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n144:     __slots__ = ('sparse',)\\n145: \\n146:     def __init__(self, sparse=False):\\n147:         self.sparse = sparse\\n148: \\n149:     def __getitem__(self, key):\\n150:         try:\\n151:             size = []\\n152:             # Mimic the behavior of `np.arange` and use a data type\\n153:             # which is at least as large as `np.int_`\\n154:             num_list = [0]\\n155:             for k in range(len(key)):\\n156:                 step = key[k].step\\n157:                 start = key[k].start\\n158:                 stop = key[k].stop\\n159:                 if start is None:\\n160:                     start = 0\\n161:                 if step is None:\\n162:                     step = 1\\n163:                 if isinstance(step, (_nx.complexfloating, complex)):\\n164:                     step = abs(step)\\n165:                     size.append(int(step))\\n166:                 else:\\n167:                     size.append(\\n168:                         int(math.ceil((stop - start) / (step*1.0))))\\n169:                 num_list += [start, stop, step]\\n170:             typ = _nx.result_type(*num_list)\\n171:             if self.sparse:\\n172:                 nn = [_nx.arange(_x, dtype=_t)\\n173:                       for _x, _t in zip(size, (typ,)*len(size))]\\n174:             else:\\n175:                 nn = _nx.indices(size, typ)\\n176:             for k, kk in enumerate(key):\\n177:                 step = kk.step\\n178:                 start = kk.start\\n179:                 if start is None:\\n180:                     start = 0\\n181:                 if step is None:\\n182:                     step = 1\\n183:                 if isinstance(step, (_nx.complexfloating, complex)):\\n184:                     step = int(abs(step))\\n185:                     if step != 1:\\n186:                         step = (kk.stop - start) / float(step - 1)\\n187:                 nn[k] = (nn[k]*step+start)\\n188:             if self.sparse:\\n189:                 slobj = [_nx.newaxis]*len(size)\\n190:                 for k in range(len(size)):\\n191:                     slobj[k] = slice(None, None)\\n192:                     nn[k] = nn[k][tuple(slobj)]\\n193:                     slobj[k] = _nx.newaxis\\n194:                 return tuple(nn)  # ogrid -> tuple of arrays\\n195:             return nn  # mgrid -> ndarray\\n196:         except (IndexError, TypeError):\\n197:             step = key.step\\n198:             stop = key.stop\\n199:             start = key.start\\n200:             if start is None:\\n201:                 start = 0\\n202:             if isinstance(step, (_nx.complexfloating, complex)):\\n203:                 # Prevent the (potential) creation of integer arrays\\n204:                 step_float = abs(step)\\n205:                 step = length = int(step_float)\\n206:                 if step != 1:\\n207:                     step = (key.stop-start)/float(step-1)\\n208:                 typ = _nx.result_type(start, stop, step_float)\\n209:                 return _nx.arange(0, length, 1, dtype=typ)*step + start\\n210:             else:\\n211:                 return _nx.arange(start, stop, step)\\n212: \\n213: \\n214: class MGridClass(nd_grid):\\n215:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n216:     An instance which returns a dense multi-dimensional \\\"meshgrid\\\".\\n217: \\n218:     An instance which returns a dense (or fleshed out) mesh-grid\\n219:     when indexed, so that each returned argument has the same shape.\\n220:     The dimensions and number of the output arrays are equal to the\\n221:     number of indexing dimensions.  If the step length is not a complex\\n222:     number, then the stop is not inclusive.\\n223: \\n224:     However, if the step length is a **complex number** (e.g. 5j), then\\n225:     the integer part of its magnitude is interpreted as specifying the\\n226:     number of points to create between the start and stop values, where\\n227:     the stop value **is inclusive**.\\n228: \\n229:     Returns\\n230:     -------\\n231:     mesh-grid : ndarray\\n232:         A single array, containing a set of `ndarray`\\\\\\\\ s all of the same\\n233:         dimensions. stacked along the first axis.\\n234: \\n235:     See Also\\n236:     --------\\n237:     ogrid : like `mgrid` but returns open (not fleshed out) mesh grids\\n238:     meshgrid: return coordinate matrices from coordinate vectors\\n239:     r_ : array concatenator\\n240:     :ref:`how-to-partition`\\n241: \\n242:     Examples\\n243:     --------\\n244:     >>> import numpy as np\\n245:     >>> np.mgrid[0:5, 0:5]\\n246:     array([[[0, 0, 0, 0, 0],\\n247:             [1, 1, 1, 1, 1],\\n248:             [2, 2, 2, 2, 2],\\n249:             [3, 3, 3, 3, 3],\\n250:             [4, 4, 4, 4, 4]],\\n251:            [[0, 1, else:\\n380:                     newobj = _nx.arange(start, stop, step)\\n381:                 if ndmin > 1:\\n382:                     newobj = array(newobj, copy=None, ndmin=ndmin)\\n383:                     if trans1d != -1:\\n384:                         newobj = newobj.swapaxes(-1, trans1d)\\n385:             elif isinstance(item, str):\\n386:                 if k != 0:\\n387: (ValueError, TypeError) as e:\\n408:                     raise ValueError(\\\"unknown special directive\\\") from e\\n409:             elif type(item) in ScalarType:\\n410:                 scalar = True\\n411:                 newobj = item\\n412:             else:\\n413:                 item_ndim = np.ndim(item)\\n414:                 newobj = array(item, copy=None, subok=True, ndmin=ndmin)\\n415:                 if trans1d != -1 and item_ndim < ndmin:\\n416:                     k2 = ndmin - item_ndim\\n417:                     k1 = trans1d\\n418:                     if k1 < r_ = concatenator(0),\\n451: # etc. because otherwise we couldn't get the doc string to come out right\\n452: # in help(r_)\\n453: \\n454: \\n455: class RClass(AxisConcatenator):\\n456:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n457:     Translates slice objects to concatenation along the first axis.\\n458: \\n459:     This is a simple way to build up arrays quickly. There are two use cases.\\n460: \\n461:     1. If the index expression contains comma separated arrays, then stack\\n462:        them along their first axis.\\n463:     2. If the index expression contains slice notation or scalars then create\\n464:        a 1-D array with a range indicated by the slice notation.\\n465: \\n466:     If slice notation is used, the syntax ``start:stop:step`` is equivalent\\n467:     to ``np.arange(start, stop, step)`` inside of the brackets. However, if\\n468:     ``step`` is an imaginary number (i.e. 100j) then its integer portion is\\n469:     interpreted as a number-of-points desired and the start and stop are\\n470:     inclusive. In other words ``start:stop:stepj`` is interpreted as\\n471:     ``np.linspace(start, stop, step, endpoint=1)`` inside of the brackets.\\n472:     After expansion of slice notation, all comma separated sequences are\\n473:     concatenated together.\\n474: \\n475:     Optional character strings placed as the first element of the index\\n476:     expression can be used to change the output. The strings 'r' or 'c' result\\n477:     in matrix output. If the result is 1-D and 'r' is specified a 1 x N (row)\\n478:     matrix is produced. If the result is 1-D and 'c' is specified, then a N x 1\\n479:     (column) matrix is produced. If the result is 2-D then both provide the\\n480:     same matrix result.\\n481: \\n482:     A string integer specifies which axis to stack multiple comma separated\\n483:     arrays along. A string of two comma-separated integers allows indication\\n484:     of the minimum number of dimensions to force each entry into as the\\n485:     second integer (the axis to concatenate along is still the first integer).\\n486: \\n487:     A string with three comma-separated integers allows specification of the\\n488:     axis to concatenate along, the minimum number of dimensions to force the\\n489:     entries to, and which axis should contain the start of the arrays which\\n490:     are less than the specified number of dimensions. In other words the third\\n491:     integer allows you to specify where the 1's should be placed in the shape\\n492:     of the arrays that have their shapes upgraded. By default, they are placed\\n493:     in the front of the shape tuple. The third argument allows you to specify\\n494:     where the start of the array should be instead. Thus, a third argument of\\n495:     '0' would place the 1's at the end of the array shape. Negative integers\\n496:     specify where in the new shape tuple the last dimension of upgraded arrays\\n497:     should be placed, so the default is '-1'.\\n498: \\n499:     Parameters\\n500:     ----------\\n501:     Not a function, so takes no parameters\\n502: \\n503: \\n504:     Returns\\n505:     -------\\n506:     A concatenated ndarray or matrix.\\n507: \\n508:     See Also\\n509:     --------\\n510:     concatenate : Join a sequence of arrays along an existing axis.\\n511:     c_ : Translates slice objects to concatenation along the second axis.\\n512: \\n513:     Examples\\n514:     --------\\n515:     >>> import numpy as np\\n516:     >>> np.r_[np.array([1,2,3]), 0, 0, np.array([4,5,6])]\\n517:     array([1, 2, 3, ..., 4, 5, 6])\\n518:     >>> np.r_[-1:1:6j, [0]*3, 5, 6]\\n519:     array([-1. , -0.6, -0.2,  0.2,  0.6,  1. ,  0. ,  0. ,  0. ,  5. ,  6. ])\\n520: \\n521:     String integers specify the axis to concatenate along or the minimum\\n522:     number of dimensions to force entries into.\\n523: \\n524:     >>> a = np.array([[0, 1, 2], [3, 4, 5]])\\n525:     >>> np.r_['-1', a, a] # concatenate along last axis\\n526:     array([[0, 1, 2, 0, 1, 2],\\n527:            [3, 4, 5, 3, 4, 5]])\\n528:     >>> np.r_['0,2', [1,2,3], [4,5,6]] # concatenate along first axis, dim>=2\\n529:     array([[1, 2, 3],\\n530:            [4, 5, 6]])\\n531: \\n532:     >>> np.r_['0,2,0', [1,2,3], [4,5,6]]\\n533:     array([[1],\\n534:            [2],\\n535:            [3],\\n536:            [4],\\n537:            [5],\\n538:            [6]])\\n539:     >>> np.r_['1,2,0', [1,2,3], [4,5,6]]\\n540:     array([[1, 4],\\n541:            [2, 5],\\n542:            [3, 6]])\\n543: \\n544:     Using 'r' or 'c' as a first string argument creates a matrix.\\n545: \\n546:     >>> np.r_['r',[1,2,3], [4,5,6]]\\n547:     matrix([[1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]])\\n548: \\n549:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n550:     __slots__ = ()\\n551: \\n552:     def __init__(self):\\n553:         AxisConcatenator.__init__(self, 0)\\n554: \\n555: \\n556: r_ = RClass()\\n557: \\n558: \\n559: class CClass(AxisConcatenator):\\n560:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n561:     Translates slice objects to concatenation along the second axis.\\n562: \\n563:     This is short-hand for ``np.r_['-1,2,0', index expression]``, which is\\n564:     useful because of its common occurrence. In particular, arrays will be\\n565:     stacked along their last axis after being upgraded to at least 2-D with\\n566:     1's post-pended to the shape (column vectors made out of 1-D arrays).\\n567: \\n568:     See Also\\n569:     --------\\n570:     column_stack : Stack 1-D arrays as columns into a 2-D array.\\n571:     r_ : For more detailed documentation.\\n572: \\n573:     Examples\\n574:     --------\\n575:     >>> import numpy as np\\n576:     >>> np.c_[np.array([1,2,3]), np.array([4,5,6])]\\n577:     array([[1, 4],\\n578:            [2, 5],\\n579:            [3, 6]])\\n580:     >>> np.c_[np.array([[1,2,3]]), 0, 0, np.array([[4,5,6]])]\\n581:     array([[1, 2, 3, ..., 4, 5, 6]])\\n582: \\n583:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n584:     __slots__ = ()\\n585: \\n586:     def     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n596:     Multidimensional index iterator.\\n597: \\n598:     Return an iterator yielding pairs of array coordinates and values.\\n599: \\n600:     Parameters\\n601: ndindex:\\n645:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n646:     An N-dimensional iterator object to index arrays.\\n647: \\n648:     Given the shape of an array, an `ndindex` instance iterates over\\n649:     the N-dimensional index of the array. --------\\n660:     ndenumerate, flatiter\\n661: \\n662:     Examples\\n663:     --------\\n664:     >>> import numpy as np\\n665: \\n666:     Dimensions as individual arguments\\n667: \\n668:     >>> \\n677:     Same dimensions - but in a tuple ``(3, 2, 1)``\\n678: \\n679:     >>> for index in np.ndindex((3, 2, 1)):\\n680:     ...     print(index)\\n681:     (0, 0, 0)\\n682:     (0, 1, 0)\\n683:     (1, 0, 0)\\n684:     (1, 1, 0)\\n685:     (2, 0, 0)\\n686:     (2, 1, 0)\\n687: \\n688:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n689: \\n690:     def __init__(self, *shape):\\n691:         if len(shape) == 1 and isinstance(shape[0], tuple):\\n692:             shape = shape[0]\\n693:         x = as_strided(_nx.zeros(1), shape=shape,\\n694:                        strides=_nx.zeros_like(shape))\\n695: ndincr(self):\\n702:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n703:         Increment the multi-dimensional index by one.\\n704: \\n705:         This method is for backward compatibility only: do not use.\\n706: \\n707:         .. deprecated:: 1.20.0\\n708:             This method has been advised against since numpy 1.8.0, but only\\n709:             started emitting DeprecationWarning as of this version.\\n710:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n711:         # NumPy 1.20.0, 2020-09-08\\n712:         warnings.warn(\\n713:             \\\"`ndindex.ndincr()` is deprecated, use `next(ndindex)` instead\\\",\\n714: the index\\n720:         tuple.\\n721: \\n722:         Returns\\n723:         -------\\n724:         val : tuple of ints\\n725:             Returns a tuple containing the indices of the current\\n726:             iteration.\\n727: \\n728:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n729:         next(self._it)\\n730:         return self._it.multi_index\\n731: \\n732: \\n733: # You can do all this with slice() plus a few special objects,\\n734: # but there's a lot to remember. This version is simpler because\\n735: # it uses the standard array indexing syntax.\\n736: #\\n737: # Written by Konrad Hinsen <hinsen@cnrs-orleans.fr>\\n738: # last revision: 1999-7-23\\n739: #\\n740: # Cosmetic changes by T. Oliphant 2001\\n741: #\\n742: #\\n743: \\n744: class IndexExpression:\\n745:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n746:     A nicer way to build up index tuples for arrays.\\n747: \\n748:     .. note::\\n749:        Use one of the two predefined instances ``index_exp`` or `s_`\\n750:        rather than directly using `IndexExpression`.\\n751: \\n752:     For any index combination, including slicing and axis insertion,\\n753:     ``a[indices]`` is the same as ``a[np.index_exp[indices]]`` for any\\n754:     array `a`. However, ``np.index_exp[indices]`` can be used anywhere\\n755:     in Python code and returns a tuple of slice objects that can be\\n756:     used in the construction of complex index expressions.\\n757: \\n758:     Parameters\\n759:     ----------\\n760:     maketuple : bool\\n761:         If True, always returns a tuple.\\n762: \\n763:     See Also\\n764:     --------\\n765:     s_ : Predefined instance without tuple conversion:\\n766:        `s_ = IndexExpression(maketuple=False)`.\\n767:        The ``index_exp`` is another predefined instance that\\n768:        always returns a tuple:\\n769:        `index_exp = IndexExpression(maketuple=True)`.\\n770: \\n771:     Notes\\n772:     -----\\n773:     You can do all this with :class:`slice` plus a few special objects,\\n774:     but there's a lot to remember and this version is simpler because\\n775:     it uses the standard array indexing syntax.\\n776: \\n777:     Examples\\n778:     --------\\n779:     >>> import numpy as np\\n780:     >>> np.s_[2::2]\\n781:     slice(2, None, 2)\\n782:     >>> np.index_exp[2::2]\\n783:     (slice(2, None, 2),)\\n784: \\n785:     >>> np.array([0, 1, 2, 3, 4])[np.s_[2::2]]\\n786:     array([2, 4])\\n787: \\n788:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n789:     __slots__ = ('maketuple',)\\n790: \\n791:     def __init__(self, maketuple):\\n792:         self.maketuple = maketuple\\n793: \\n794:     def __getitem__(self, item):\\n795:         if self.maketuple and not isinstance(item, tuple):\\n796:             return (item,)\\n797:         else:\\n798:             return item\\n799: \\n800: \\n801: index_exp = IndexExpression(maketuple=True)\\n802: s_ = IndexExpression(maketuple=False)\\n803: \\n804: # End contribution from Konrad.\\n805: \\n806: \\n807: # The following functions complement those in twodim_base, but are\\n808: # applicable to N-dimensions.\\n809: \\n810: \\n811: def _fill_diagonal_dispatcher(a, val, wrap=None):\\n812:     return (a,)\\n813: \\n814: \\n815: @array_function_dispatch(_fill_diagonal_dispatcher)\\n816: def fill_diagonal(a, val, wrap=False):\\n817:     \\\"\\\"\\\"Fill the main diagonal of the given array of any dimensionality.\\n818: \\n819:     For an array `a` with ``a.ndim >= 2``, the diagonal is the list of\\n820:     values ``a[i, ..., i]`` with indices ``i`` all identical.  This function\\n821:     modifies the input array in-place without returning a value.\\n822: \\n823:     Parameters\\n824:     ----------\\n825:     a : array, at least 2-D.\\n826:       Array whose diagonal is to be filled in-place.\\n827:     val : scalar or array_like\\n828:       Value(s) to write on the diagonal. If `val` is scalar, the value is\\n829:       written along the diagonal. If array-like, the flattened `val` is\\n830:       written along the diagonal, repeating if necessary to fill all\\n831:       diagonal entries.\\n832: \\n833:     wrap : bool\\n834:       For tall matrices in NumPy version up to 1.6.2, the\\n835:       diagonal \\\"wrapped\\\" after N columns. You can have this behavior\\n836:       with this option. This affects only tall matrices.\\n837: \\n838:     See also\\n839:     --------\\n840:     diag_indices, diag_indices_from\\n841: \\n842:     Notes\\n843:     -----\\n844:     This functionality can be obtained via `diag_indices`, but internally\\n845:     this version uses a much faster implementation that never constructs the\\n846:     indices and uses simple slicing.\\n847: \\n848:     Examples\\n849:     --------\\n850:     >>> import numpy as np\\n851:     >>> a = np.zeros((3, 3), int)\\n852:     >>> np.fill_diagonal(a, 5)\\n853:     >>> a\\n854:     array([[5, 0, 0],\\n855:            [0, 5, 0],\\n856:            [0, 0, 5]])\\n857: \\n858:     The same function can operate on a 4-D array:\\n859: \\n860:     >>> a = np.zeros((3, 3, 3, 3), int)\\n861:     >>> np.fill_diagonal(a, 4)\\n862: \\n863:     We only show a few blocks for clarity:\\n864: \\n865:     >>> a[0, 0]\\n866:     array([[4, 0, 0],\\n867:            [0, 0, 0],\\n868:            [0, 0, 0]])\\n869:     >>> a[1, 1]\\n870:     array([[0, 0, 0],\\n871:            [0, 4, 0],\\n872:            [0, 0, 0]])\\n873:     >>> a[2, 2]\\n874:     array([[0, 0, 0],\\n875:            [0, 0, 0],\\n876:            [0, 0, 4]])\\n877: \\n878:     The wrap option affects only tall matrices:\\n879: \\n880:     >>> # tall matrices no wrap\\n881:     >>> a = np.zeros((5, 3), int)\\n882:     >>> np.fill_diagonal(a, 4)\\n883:     >>> a\\n884:     array([[4, 0, 0],\\n885:            [0, 4, 0],\\n886:            [0, 0, 4],\\n887:            [0, 0, 0],\\n888:            [0, 0, 0]])\\n889: \\n890:     >>> # tall matrices wrap\\n891:     >>> a = np.zeros((5, 3), int)\\n892:     >>> np.fill_diagonal(a, 4, wrap=True)\\n893:     >>> a\\n894:     array([[4, 0, 0],\\n895:            [0, 4, 0],\\n896:            [0, 0, 4],\\n897:            [0, 0, 0],\\n898:            [4, 0, 0]])\\n899: \\n900:     >>> # wide matrices\\n901:     >>> a = np.zeros((3, 5), int)\\n902:     >>> np.fill_diagonal(a, 4, wrap=True)\\n903:     >>> a\\n904:     array([[4, 0, 0, 0, 0],\\n905:            [0, 4, 0, 0, 0],\\n906:            [0, 0, 4, 0, 0]])\\n907: \\n908:     The anti-diagonal can be filled by reversing the order of elements\\n909:     using either `numpy.flipud` or `numpy.fliplr`.\\n910: \\n911:     >>> a = np.zeros((3, 3), int);\\n912:     >>> np.fill_diagonal(np.fliplr(a), [1,2,3])  # Horizontal flip\\n913:     >>> a\\n914:     array([[0, 0, 1],\\n915:            [0, 2, 0],\\n916:            [3, 0, 0]])\\n917:     >>> np.fill_diagonal(np.flipud(a), [1,2,3])  # Vertical flip\\n918:     >>> a\\n919:     array([[0, 0, 3],\\n920:            [0, 2, 0],\\n921:            [1, 0, 0]])\\n922: \\n923:     Note that the order in which the diagonal is filled varies depending\\n924:     on the flip function.\\n925:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n926:     if a.ndim < 2:\\n927:         raise ValueError(\\\"array must be at least 2-d\\\")\\n928:     end = None\\n929:     if a.ndim == 2:\\n930:         # Explicit, fast formula for the common case.  For 2-d arrays, we\\n931:         # accept rectangular ones.\\n932:         step = a.shape[1] + 1\\n933:         # This is needed to don't have tall matrix have the diagonal wrap.\\n934:         if not wrap:\\n935:             end = a.shape[1] * a.shape[1]\\n936:     else:\\n937:         # For more than d=2, the strided formula is only valid for arrays with\\n938:         # all dimensions equal, so we check first.\\n939:         if not np.all(diff(a.shape) == 0):\\n940:             raise ValueError(\\\"All dimensions of input must be of equal length\\\")\\n941:         step = 1 + (np.cumprod(a.shape[:-1])).sum()\\n942: \\n943:     # Write the value out into the diagonal.\\n944:     a.flat[:end:step] = val\\n945: \\n946: \\n947: @set_module('numpy')\\n948: def diag_indices(n, ndim=2):\\n949:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n950:     Return the indices to access the main diagonal of an array.\\n951: \\n952:     This returns a tuple of indices that can be used to access the main\\n953:     diagonal of an array `a` with ``a.ndim >= 2`` dimensions and shape\\n954:     (n, n, ..., n). For ``a.ndim = 2`` this is the usual diagonal, for\\n955:     ``a.ndim > 2`` this is the set of indices to access ``a[i, i, ..., i]``\\n956:     for ``i = [0..n-1]``.\\n957: \\n958:     Parameters\\n959:     ----------\\n960:     n : int\\n961:       The size, along each dimension, of the arrays for which the returned\\n962:       indices can be used.\\n963: \\n964:     ndim : int, as np\\n974: \\n975:     Create a set of indices to access the diagonal of a (4, 4) array:\\n976: \\n977:     >>> di = np.diag_indices(4)\\n978:     >>> di\\n979:     (array([0, 1, 2, 3]), array([0, 1, 2, 3]))\\n980:     >>> a = np.arange(16).reshape(4, 4)\\n981:     >>> a\\n982:     array([[ 0,  1,  2,  3],\\n983:            [ 4,  5,  6,  7],\\n984:            [ 8,  9, 10, 11],\\n985:            [12, 13, 14, 15]])\\n986:     >>> a[di] = 100\\n987:     >>> a\\n988:     array([[100,   1,   2,   3],\\n989:            [  4, 100,   6,   7],\\n990:            [  8,   9, 100,  11],\\n991:            [ 12,  13,  14, 100]])\\n992: \\n993:     Now, we create indices to manipulate a 3-D array:\\n994: \\n995:     >>> d3 = np.diag_indices(2, 3)\\n996:     >>> d3\\n997:     (array([0, 1]), array([0, 1]), array([0, 1]))\\n998: \\n999:     And use it to set the diagonal of an array of zeros to 1:\\n1000: \\n1001:     >>> a = np.zeros((2, 2, 2), dtype=int)\\n1002:     >>> a[d3] = 1\\n1003:     >>> {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01Cc8S4MfRQ7v1uPkUrzCyST","type":"tool_result","content":"{\"content\":\"1: \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n2: Discrete Fourier Transforms - _helper.py\\n3: \\n4: \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n5: from numpy._core import integer, empty, arange, asarray, roll\\n6: from numpy._core.overrides import array_function_dispatch, set_module\\n7: \\n8: # Created by Pearu Peterson, September 2002\\n9: \\n10: __all__ = ['fftshift', 'ifftshift', 'fftfreq', 'rfftfreq']\\n11: \\n12: integer_types = (int, integer)\\n13: \\n14: \\n15: def _fftshift_dispatcher(x, axes=None):\\n16:     return (x,)\\n17: \\n18: \\n19: @array_function_dispatch(_fftshift_dispatcher, module='numpy.fft')\\n20: def fftshift(x, axes=None):\\n21:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n22:     Shift the zero-frequency component to the center of the spectrum.\\n23: \\n24:     This function swaps half-spaces for all axes listed (defaults to all).\\n25:     Note that ``y[0]`` is the Nyquist component only if ``len(x)`` is even.\\n26: \\n27:     Parameters\\n28:     ----------\\n29:     x : array_like\\n30:         Input array.\\n31:     axes : int or shape tuple, optional\\n32:         Axes over which to shift.  Default is None, which shifts all axes.\\n33: \\n34:     Returns\\n35:     -------\\n36:     y : ndarray\\n37:         The shifted array.\\n38: \\n39:     See Also\\n40:     --------\\n41:     ifftshift : The inverse of `fftshift`.\\n42: \\n43:     Examples\\n44:     --------\\n45:     >>> import numpy as np\\n46:     >>> freqs = np.fft.fftfreq(10, 0.1)\\n47:     >>> freqs\\n48:     array([ 0.,  1.,  2., ..., -3., -2., -1.])\\n49:     >>> np.fft.fftshift(freqs)\\n50:     array([-5., -4., -3., -2., -1.,  0.,  1.,  2.,  3.,  4.])\\n51: \\n52:     Shift the zero-frequency component only along the second axis:\\n53: \\n54:     >>> freqs = np.fft.fftfreq(9, d=1./9).reshape(3, 3)\\n55:     >>> freqs\\n56:     array([[ 0.,  1.,  2.],\\n57:            [ 3.,  4., -4.],\\n58:            [-3., -2., -1.]])\\n59:     >>> np.fft.fftshift(freqs, axes=(1,))\\n60:     array([[ 2.,  0.,  1.],\\n61:            [-4.,  3.,  4.],\\n62:            [-1., -3., -2.]])\\n63: \\n64:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n65:     x = asarray(x)\\n66:     if axes is None:\\n67:         axes = tuple(range(x.ndim))\\n68:         shift = [dim // 2 for dim in x.shape]\\n69:     elif isinstance(axes, integer_types):\\n70:         shift = x.shape[axes] // 2\\n71:     else:\\n72:         shift = [x.shape[ax] // 2 for ax in axes]\\n73: \\n74:     return roll(x, shift, axes)\\n75: \\n76: \\n77: @array_function_dispatch(_fftshift_dispatcher, module='numpy.fft')\\n78: def ifftshift(x, axes=None):\\n79:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n80:     The inverse of `fftshift`. Although identical for even-length `x`, the\\n81:     functions differ by one sample for odd-length `x`.\\n82: \\n83:     Parameters\\n84:     ----------\\n85:     x : array_like\\n86:         Input array.\\n87:     axes : int or shape tuple, optional\\n88:         Axes over which to calculate.  Defaults to None, which shifts all axes.\\n89: \\n90:     Returns\\n91:     -------\\n92:     y : ndarray\\n93:         The shifted array.\\n94: \\n95:     See Also\\n96:     --------\\n97:     fftshift : Shift zero-frequency component to the center of the spectrum.\\n98: \\n99:     Examples\\n100:     --------\\n101:     >>> import numpy as np\\n102:     >>> freqs = np.fft.fftfreq(9, d=1./9).reshape(3, 3)\\n103:     >>> freqs\\n104:     array([[ 0.,  1.,  2.],\\n105:            [ 3.,  4., -4.],\\n106:            [-3., -2., -1.]])\\n107:     >>> np.fft.ifftshift(np.fft.fftshift(freqs))\\n108:     array([[ 0.,  1.,  2.],\\n109:            [ 3.,  4., -4.],\\n110:            [-3., -2., -1.]])\\n111: \\n112:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n113:     x = asarray(x)\\n114:     if axes is None:\\n115:         axes = tuple(range(x.ndim))\\n116:         shift = [-(dim // 2) for dim in x.shape]\\n117:     elif isinstance(axes, integer_types):\\n118:         shift = -(x.shape[axes] // 2)\\n119:     else:\\n120:         shift = [-(x.shape[ax] // 2) for ax in axes]\\n121: \\n122:     return roll(x, shift, axes)\\n123: \\n124: \\n125: @set_module('numpy.fft')\\n126: def fftfreq(n, d=1.0, device=None):\\n127:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n128:     Return the Discrete Fourier Transform sample frequencies.\\n129: \\n130:     The returned float array `f` contains the frequency bin centers in cycles\\n131:     per unit of the sample spacing (with zero at the start).  For instance, if\\n132:     the sample spacing is in seconds, then the frequency unit is cycles/second.\\n133: \\n134:     Given a window length `n` and a sample spacing `d`::\\n135: \\n136:       f = [0, 1, ...,   n/2-1,     -n/2, ..., -1] / (d*n)   if n is even\\n137:       f = [0, 1, ..., (n-1)/2, -(n-1)/2, ..., -1] / (d*n)   if n is odd\\n138: \\n139:     Parameters\\n140:     ----------\\n141:     n : int\\n142:         Window length.\\n143:     d : scalar, optional\\n144:         Sample spacing (inverse of the sampling rate). Defaults to 1.\\n145:     device : str, optional\\n146:         The device on which to place the created array. Default: ``None``.\\n147:         For Array-API interoperability only, so must be ``\\\"cpu\\\"`` if passed.\\n148: \\n149:         .. versionadded:: 2.0.0\\n150: \\n151:     Returns\\n152:     -------\\n153:     f : ndarray\\n154:         Array of length `n` containing the sample frequencies.\\n155: \\n156:     Examples\\n157:     --------\\n158:     >>> import numpy as np\\n159:     >>> signal = np.array([-2, 8, 6, 4, 1, 0, 3, 5], dtype=float)\\n160:     >>> fourier = np.fft.fft(signal)\\n161:     >>> n = signal.size\\n162:     >>> timestep = 0.1\\n163:     >>> freq = np.fft.fftfreq(n, d=timestep)\\n164:     >>> freq\\n165:     array([ 0.  ,  1.25,  2.5 , ..., -3.75, -2.5 , -1.25])\\n166: \\n167:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n168:     if not isinstance(n, integer_types):\\n169:         raise ValueError(\\\"n should be an integer\\\")\\n170:     val = 1.0 / (n * d)\\n171:     results = empty(n, int, device=device)\\n172:     N = (n-1)//2 + 1\\n173:     p1 = arange(0, N, dtype=int, device=device)\\n174:     results[:N] = p1\\n175:     p2 = arange(-(n//2), 0, dtype=int, device=device)\\n176:     results[N:] = p2\\n177:     return results * val\\n178: \\n179: \\n180: @set_module('numpy.fft')\\n181: def rfftfreq(n, d=1.0, device=None):\\n182:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n183:     Return the Discrete Fourier Transform sample frequencies\\n184:     (for usage with rfft, irfft).\\n185: \\n186:     The returned float array `f` contains the frequency bin centers in cycles\\n187:     per unit of the sample spacing (with zero at the start).  For instance, if\\n188:     the sample spacing is in seconds, then the frequency unit is cycles/second.\\n189: \\n190:     Given a window length `n` and a sample spacing `d`::\\n191: \\n192:       f = [0, 1, ...,     n/2-1,     n/2] / (d*n)   if n is even\\n193:       f = [0, 1, ..., (n-1)/2-1, (n-1)/2] / (d*n)   if n is odd\\n194: \\n195:     Unlike `fftfreq` (but like `scipy.fftpack.rfftfreq`)\\n196:     the Nyquist frequency component is considered to be positive.\\n197: \\n198:     Parameters\\n199:     ----------\\n200:     n : int\\n201:         Window length.\\n202:     d : scalar, optional\\n203:         Sample spacing (inverse of the sampling rate). Defaults to 1.\\n204:     device : str, optional\\n205:         The device on which to place the created array. Default: ``None``.\\n206:         For Array-API interoperability only, so must be ``\\\"cpu\\\"`` if passed.\\n207: \\n208:         .. versionadded:: 2.0.0\\n209: \\n210:     Returns\\n211:     -------\\n212:     f : ndarray\\n213:         Array of length ``n//2 + 1`` containing the sample frequencies.\\n214: \\n215:     Examples\\n216:     --------\\n217:     >>> import numpy as np\\n218:     >>> signal = np.array([-2, 8, 6, 4, 1, 0, 3, 5, -3, 4], dtype=float)\\n219:     >>> fourier = np.fft.rfft(signal)\\n220:     >>> n = signal.size\\n221:     >>> sample_rate = 100\\n222:     >>> freq = np.fft.fftfreq(n, d=1./sample_rate)\\n223:     >>> freq\\n224:     array([  0.,  10.,  20., ..., -30., -20., -10.])\\n225:     >>> freq = np.fft.rfftfreq(n, d=1./sample_rate)\\n226:     >>> freq\\n227:     array([  0.,  10.,  20.,  30.,  40.,  50.])\\n228: \\n229:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n230:     if not isinstance(n, integer_types):\\n231:         raise ValueError(\\\"n should be an integer\\\")\\n232:     val = 1.0/(n*d)\\n233:     N = n//2 + 1\\n234:     results = arange(0, N, dtype=int, device=device)\\n235:     return results * val\\n236: \",\"url\":\"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/numpy--v2.2.2@9ca88bff35bf60de74f9f95e6f90e6b4e0913e78/-/blob/numpy/fft/_helper.py\"}"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":"toolu_01KcrzQMt8QSgtWj8vuYnku5","session_id":"3818d021-ff76-4cbe-9aef-54b9f8949d86","uuid":"3b6bd8de-a6ce-4584-a009-04cc3f1341ed"}

It has better\n81:     performance for large data than Sturges, but tends to overestimate\n82:     the number of bins. The number of bins is proportional to the cube\n83:     root of data size (asymptotically optimal). The estimate depends\n84:     only on size of the data.\n85: \n86:     Parameters\n87:     ----------\n88:     x : array_like\n89:         Input data that is to be histogrammed, trimmed to range. May not\n90:         be empty.\n91: \n92:     Returns\n93:     -------\n94:     h : An estimate of the optimal bin width for the given data.\n95:     \"\"\"\n96:     del range  # unused\n97:     return _ptp(x) / (2.0 * x.size ** (1.0 / 3))\n98: \n99: \n100: def _hist_bin_scott(x, range):\n101: ","url":"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/numpy--v2.2.2@9ca88bff35bf60de74f9f95e6f90e6b4e0913e78/-/blob/numpy/lib/_histograms_impl.py%3FL1-100"}}}

Returns\\n101:     -------\\n102:     new_result : set\\n103:         The indices of the resulting contraction\\n104:     remaining : list\\n105:         List of sets that have not been contracted, The indices used in the current contraction\\n111: \\n112:     Examples\\n113:     --------\\n114: \\n115:     # A simple dot product test case\\n116:     >>> pos = (0, 1)\\n117:     >>> isets =
